title: Strait of Hormuz Closure
subtitle: US/Israeli strikes trigger IRGC closure — P&I clubs cancel war risk coverage
summary: Following US-Israeli airstrikes on IRGC naval facilities at Bandar Abbas, Iran formally declared the Strait of Hormuz closed on 4 March. Seven P&I clubs issued coordinated 48-hour war risk cancellation notices — the first multi-club action since 2019. Transit volumes are down 80%. 150 vessels stranded. The closure is actuarial, not military: no insured vessel can transit without valid cover.

risk_compliance.headline: Immediate sanctions and war risk compliance exposure across all Gulf operations
risk_compliance.detail: The IRGC closure declaration and ongoing OFAC Iran sanctions create complex legal exposure. Any cover provided to vessels in breach of closure creates regulatory risk. Review all existing Gulf hull and cargo policies for war risk endorsements. The DFC backstop facility creates a potential gap between OFAC-sanctioned Iranian conduct and US-sponsored cover mechanisms.
actions: Review all Arabian Gulf vessel policies for war risk clause status and endorsement currency
Assess whether DFC backstop facility creates OFAC compliance obligations before using
File incident notification with QCB within 72 hours per Circular 2023-INS-07
Document all coverage decisions with sanctions screening evidence

claims.headline: Multiple vessel strike claims imminent — GA and war risk recovery complexity ahead
claims.detail: MV Nova Spirit (VLCC, 298k DWT) is the first confirmed major loss. General Average may be declared. War risk cancellation notices mean subsequent claims may face coverage disputes. The 48-hour notice period creates a precise liability boundary. Claims filed after notice expiry but arising from pre-notice incidents require careful date analysis.
actions: Open reserve file for MV Nova Spirit immediately — GA declaration likely
Establish cut-off timestamp analysis for all Gulf vessel claims vs P&I notice periods
Identify all open Gulf hull/cargo claims and segregate pre/post-cancellation incidents
Appoint specialist war risk solicitors for anticipated coverage disputes

underwriting.headline: Suspend new Gulf war risk business immediately — repricing required across portfolio
underwriting.detail: The P&I coordinated cancellation has effectively destroyed the war risk market for Gulf transits. Any new business written now carries unquantifiable accumulation risk without a functioning reinsurance backstop. The DFC facility covers US-flagged vessels only. All open quotations and renewal terms for Gulf hull and cargo should be suspended pending market stabilisation.
actions: Suspend all new Arabian Gulf war risk quotations effective immediately
Issue portfolio-wide hold on all Gulf renewal terms pending review
Calculate aggregate probable maximum loss across current Gulf hull portfolio
Assess DFC backstop eligibility for US-flagged vessels in portfolio

reinsurance.headline: Accumulation event confirmed — treaty cat provisions almost certainly triggered
reinsurance.detail: The Hormuz closure constitutes a single accumulation event across marine hull, cargo and energy lines. Treaty cat provisions should be reviewed immediately. The 80% transit volume reduction means exposure is concentrated in the 20% still transiting or stranded. Retrocession market is equally distressed.
actions: Trigger accumulation monitoring protocol — event reference date: 04 March 2026
Review all treaty wordings for Gulf accumulation caps and event definitions
Notify lead reinsurers per treaty notification obligations (typically 30-day window)
Model PML scenarios: 10 vessel strikes / 50 vessel strikes / 150 stranded total loss

investments.headline: Gulf sovereign credit and shipping equity exposure requires immediate review
investments.detail: The Hormuz closure is a major negative shock to Gulf sovereign credit. Qatar, UAE and Kuwait depend on Hormuz for LNG and crude export revenues. Energy equity prices have spiked on supply shock premium but are vulnerable to demand destruction. Fixed income: avoid new Gulf sovereign issuance until clarity on duration.
actions: Review Gulf sovereign bond positions — Qatar, UAE, Kuwait all Hormuz-dependent
Reduce or hedge shipping equity exposure — war risk premium spike not sustainable
Monitor LNG spot price for impact on Qatar sovereign credit
Assess reinsurance-linked securities exposure to Gulf marine events

title: Red Sea War Risk Market Collapse
subtitle: 26-month Houthi attrition depletes war risk capital — cascade market exit underway
summary: The Red Sea war risk market has reached structural collapse after 26 months of continuous Houthi attrition from November 2023. Lloyd's war risk capital pool is effectively exhausted with premiums at 20x baseline. 14 syndicates have triggered Solvency II exit provisions. Houthi attacks resumed March 1. Red Sea cargo transits are at 35% of 2023 levels.

risk_compliance.headline: Solvency II capital adequacy triggers require regulatory notification
risk_compliance.detail: The cascade exit of Lloyd's syndicates under Solvency II provisions creates reporting obligations. Any Qatar-regulated entity with Red Sea war risk exposure must assess whether capital adequacy ratios remain compliant with QCB solvency requirements.
actions: Assess capital adequacy ratios against QCB minimum requirements
File quarterly prudential return update if Red Sea exposure has materially changed
Review reinsurance panel for any syndicates in exit — replace capacity immediately

claims.headline: Cargo claims surge anticipated — policy trigger and valuation disputes likely
claims.detail: 26 months of attrition has created a significant backlog of disputed cargo claims. With market exits and premium disputes, many policyholders are arguing cover was maintained despite non-payment. Expect surge in claims notifications as shipping diverts.
actions: Audit all Red Sea cargo claims for payment status and coverage continuity
Flag claims where premium disputes may affect policy validity
Establish specialist adjusting team for Red Sea cargo claims volume

underwriting.headline: Red Sea war risk appetite must be suspended — no viable reinsurance backstop
underwriting.detail: With the LMA capital pool exhausted and GIC Re withdrawn, writing new Red Sea war risk without reinsurance is a balance sheet decision, not an underwriting decision. Any new business must be approved at board level with full net retention analysis.
actions: Escalate Red Sea war risk appetite decision to board level
Calculate maximum net retention acceptable given current capital position
Identify alternative reinsurance capacity — mutual/governmental facilities

reinsurance.headline: Treaty exhaustion — Red Sea event represents aggregate cat threshold breach
reinsurance.detail: The 26-month event sequence means most marine reinsurance treaties will have exhausted annual aggregate sub-limits for Red Sea war risk. The Houthi resumption post-Hormuz creates a new event sequence question: continuation vs new cat event.
actions: Determine event sequencing with lead reinsurers — continuation vs new event
Model aggregate exhaustion across all marine treaties
Prepare for difficult renewal — retrocession market severely distressed

investments.headline: Suez revenue collapse damages Egyptian sovereign credit — regional contagion risk
investments.detail: Suez Canal revenues are down 70% year-on-year. Egypt's $35bn IMF programme assumes Suez revenues of $8bn annually. With revenues at $2.4bn run-rate, fiscal pressure is building.
actions: Review Egyptian sovereign bond exposure — fiscal stress is material
Assess regional port and shipping equity positions
Monitor Egypt IMF programme compliance metrics

title: Russia-Ukraine Ceasefire Negotiations
subtitle: Dual-track risk: ongoing conflict disruption vs $500bn+ reconstruction opportunity
summary: Two parallel diplomatic tracks are active: the Coalition of the Willing Paris Declaration (6 Jan) and the US-Russia-Ukraine Abu Dhabi framework talks (23 Jan). Simultaneously, Russia continues kinetic operations including a 100+ drone strike on Kyiv during the Abu Dhabi session. The risk is bilateral — continued conflict sustains existing losses; successful ceasefire opens reconstruction.

risk_compliance.headline: OFAC Ukraine/Russia sanctions remain active — ceasefire does not lift them
risk_compliance.detail: Existing OFAC sanctions on Russian entities remain fully in force regardless of ceasefire status. Any reconstruction-related insurance activity must be sanctions-screened. The Paris Declaration creates potential exposure for European companies involved in security force deployment.
actions: Maintain OFAC screening for all Russia-adjacent transactions
Monitor Paris Declaration signatories for sanctions compliance implications
Brief compliance team: ceasefire is diplomatic, sanctions relief requires legislation

claims.headline: Property claims pipeline in Ukraine — war damage valuation standards unresolved
claims.detail: There is no agreed international standard for war damage property valuation in Ukraine. IUMI and Lloyd's have competing approaches. The Kyiv drone strike adds to the open claims pipeline. Ceasefire would accelerate claims notification but create reconstruction cost inflation.
actions: Establish valuation methodology for Ukrainian property claims now
Engage with IUMI on emerging war damage standard
Open provisional reserves for Kyiv drone strike — building damage assessments pending

underwriting.headline: Reconstruction opportunity is real — begin capacity analysis before ceasefire announcement
underwriting.detail: $500bn+ reconstruction requirement will need insurance capacity for property, engineering, liability and political risk. The window to build expertise and relationships is before the announcement. Post-announcement, the market will be overwhelmed and terms will harden.
actions: Begin reconstruction insurance capacity analysis for property and engineering lines
Engage with EBRD and World Bank on reconstruction financing insurance requirements
Develop war damage exclusion carve-back product for reconstruction scenarios

reinsurance.headline: Ukraine reconstruction requires specialist cat-scale capacity — begin modelling now
reinsurance.detail: The scale of Ukrainian infrastructure damage ($500bn+) exceeds current property reinsurance cat modelling frameworks. New models are needed. Begin conversations with cat modellers before the rush.
actions: Commission Ukraine reconstruction PML analysis
Engage cat modellers on conflict reconstruction peril modelling
Assess retrocession appetite for Eastern European property cat capacity

investments.headline: Ceasefire is the single largest positive catalyst in European credit since COVID recovery
investments.detail: A Ukraine ceasefire would be the most significant positive catalyst for European credit markets since 2021. European construction, defence, infrastructure and energy equities would re-rate significantly. Polish and Baltic sovereign credit would tighten.
actions: Increase allocation to European reconstruction thematic equities (construction, infrastructure, energy)
Review Central/Eastern European sovereign bond positions for ceasefire upside
Hedge against downside scenario (continued conflict) with European energy exposure

title: Trump Tariff Regime — Supply Chain Disruption
subtitle: 25.5% average US tariff — highest since 1890s — insolvency wave forecast
summary: The Trump administration has imposed a 25.5% average tariff rate, with China at 130% and retaliatory measures from EU, Canada and Mexico. Allianz Trade forecasts US insolvency +16% 2026, global +7%. IEEPA rare earth controls on 7 critical minerals. SCOTUS IEEPA ruling pending Q2 2026 — 61% of increase at risk.

risk_compliance.headline: IEEPA rare earth controls create new sanctions-equivalent compliance obligations
risk_compliance.detail: The IEEPA rare earth export controls have sanctions-equivalent effect. Companies in supply chains using neodymium, dysprosium or terbium must verify OFAC compliance on all counterparty transactions. Maintain compliance programmes even given SCOTUS uncertainty.
actions: Conduct supply chain mapping for IEEPA-controlled minerals in policyholder portfolios
Update AML/KYC procedures to include rare earth supply chain exposure
Brief underwriting teams on IEEPA compliance obligations for credit insurance

claims.headline: Business interruption and trade credit claims will spike with insolvency wave
claims.detail: Allianz Trade's +16% US insolvency forecast will translate directly to credit insurance and trade credit claims. Supply chain disruption creates BI trigger questions — tariff-related production halts may not meet standard BI definitions.
actions: Review credit insurance and trade credit policy wordings for tariff/insolvency triggers
Establish early warning protocol for policyholder credit deterioration
Identify top 20 US-exposed credit insurance policyholders by revenue concentration

underwriting.headline: Credit and trade credit require immediate repricing — insolvency assumptions are stale
underwriting.detail: All credit insurance pricing models pre-dating February 2026 use insolvency assumptions that are materially wrong. +16% US and +7% global uplift must be applied. New business should be paused pending repricing.
actions: Suspend credit insurance renewal terms pending insolvency assumption update
Apply +16% US and +7% global insolvency stress to all credit pricing models
Review top 10 US-exposed accounts for material adverse change

reinsurance.headline: Credit reinsurance aggregate will breach if insolvency forecast materialises
reinsurance.detail: A +16% US insolvency environment would likely breach annual aggregate sub-limits in credit reinsurance treaties. Model current portfolio against the Allianz Trade scenario now before renewals.
actions: Model credit reinsurance aggregate exhaustion under Allianz Trade insolvency scenario
Notify lead credit reinsurers of portfolio repositioning
Review excess of loss credit treaties for annual aggregate reinstatement provisions

investments.headline: Tariff regime is the dominant macro risk for global fixed income and equities in 2026
investments.detail: The combination of tariff inflation, supply disruption and insolvency wave creates stagflationary pressure. Investment grade credit spreads will widen. US equities face earnings headwinds. Rare earth controls create specific sector impacts for EV, defence and semiconductors.
actions: Reduce US investment grade credit exposure — spread widening likely
Avoid consumer discretionary and manufacturing equities with high tariff pass-through
Increase allocation to commodity-linked assets as supply disruption premium builds

title: Iran-Linked Cyber Escalation
subtitle: APT operations coordinated with kinetic Hormuz campaign — SCADA/energy targets
summary: CISA has identified elevated Iranian APT (UNC3890/IRIDIUM) activity coordinated with the kinetic Hormuz campaign. Primary targets are SCADA/ICS systems in Gulf energy infrastructure. GPS spoofing is affecting 1,000+ vessels. CISA workforce reduced 17% with director vacancy at a critical moment.

risk_compliance.headline: QCB Cyber Security Regulation compliance review required — active threat environment
risk_compliance.detail: The QCB Insurance Sector Cyber Security Regulation requires insurers to maintain incident response plans and report material cyber incidents within 24 hours. The CISA advisory constitutes a material threat to Gulf-based operations. GPS spoofing affecting vessels may be a covered cyber event under existing policies.
actions: Convene cyber incident response team per QCB Cyber Security Regulation requirements
Review GPS spoofing exposure in marine hull portfolio — potential navigation incident trigger
File threat awareness notification with QCB per circular reporting obligations
Verify SCADA security controls for any energy facility policyholders

claims.headline: GPS spoofing creating navigation incident exposure — review all Gulf hull policies now
claims.detail: GPS spoofing of 1,000+ vessels creates potential navigation incident triggers across the marine hull portfolio. Vessels using compromised GPS data face collision and grounding risk. This is a novel cyber-kinetic intersection claim — policy wording analysis is critical for cyber exclusions vs navigation perils.
actions: Identify all open navigation incident claims with Gulf waypoints since 20 Feb 2026
Review marine hull cyber exclusion clauses for GPS spoofing applicability
Engage specialist cyber marine adjusters for hybrid cyber-navigation claims

underwriting.headline: Cyber-marine hybrid risk requires revised underwriting guidelines
underwriting.detail: The GPS spoofing campaign illustrates that cyber risk and marine hull risk are no longer separable in the Gulf. Standard marine hull policies with cyber exclusions may leave significant uninsured losses.
actions: Review all Gulf marine hull policies for cyber exclusion clause adequacy
Develop cyber-marine hybrid underwriting guidelines
Suspend new Gulf energy infrastructure cyber cover pending threat assessment

reinsurance.headline: Cyber-kinetic accumulation — coordinate with marine reinsurers on event definition
reinsurance.detail: If GPS spoofing losses are classified as cyber events, they aggregate under cyber reinsurance treaties. If navigation incidents, under marine. The dual-classification risk creates accumulation blind spots.
actions: Obtain lead reinsurer guidance on GPS spoofing event classification
Model cyber-marine accumulation scenario under both classification approaches
Review silent cyber provisions in marine reinsurance treaties

investments.headline: Energy infrastructure cyber risk is an underpriced tail — review ILS exposure
investments.detail: The coordination between kinetic and cyber attacks on Gulf energy infrastructure represents a systemic risk that energy equity valuations do not currently reflect.
actions: Review ILS portfolio for Gulf energy cyber exposure
Assess energy equity positions for cyber infrastructure vulnerability
Monitor Iranian APT activity as leading indicator for energy price volatility

title: Sudan Civil War — Port Sudan Corridor Threat
subtitle: RSF advance on Port Sudan threatens sole humanitarian corridor
summary: Rapid Support Forces have advanced within 40km of Port Sudan, threatening the only viable humanitarian and commercial entry point for Sudan. WFP reports 4.9 million people in Darfur at famine risk. The situation is compounded by elevated shipping costs from the Red Sea/Hormuz crises.

risk_compliance.headline: Sudan sanctions and Darfur humanitarian exception compliance review
risk_compliance.detail: OFAC Sudan sanctions include specific humanitarian exemptions. Verify that any humanitarian cargo insurance or Port Sudan port operations comply with applicable exemption requirements.
actions: Review Sudan sanctions compliance for any Port Sudan-related policies
Verify humanitarian exemption applicability for WFP or NGO cargo cover
Monitor OFAC for new RSF-targeted designations

claims.headline: Marine cargo and humanitarian supply chain claims will increase with corridor disruption
claims.detail: Port Sudan closure or disruption would create significant cargo claims from humanitarian and commercial shipments. Cargo in transit to Port Sudan would face diversion, delay or total loss.
actions: Identify all open cargo policies with Port Sudan as destination
Establish contingency reserve for Port Sudan corridor disruption scenario
Brief adjusters on political risk vs marine cargo claim allocation for Sudan losses

underwriting.headline: Sudan political violence exclusions require review — conflict scope has widened
underwriting.detail: The RSF offensive represents significant escalation from the 2023 Khartoum conflict. Political violence exclusions written in 2023 may not adequately capture the current geographic scope now reaching Port Sudan.
actions: Review all Sudan political violence exclusion geographic scope
Pause new Port Sudan-destination cargo quotations
Assess whether marine accumulation in Port Sudan area exceeds comfort level

reinsurance.headline: Sudan conflict unlikely to trigger cat treaty — accumulation monitoring only
reinsurance.detail: Sudan losses are likely to remain below cat treaty threshold. However, compounding of Port Sudan disruption with Red Sea and Hormuz creates an unusual accumulation scenario in the humanitarian cargo line.
actions: Monitor Port Sudan accumulation against per-location sub-limits
Assess interaction with Red Sea war risk aggregate exhaustion

investments.headline: Sudan is a secondary risk — monitor for humanitarian corridor impact on aid flows
investments.detail: Sudan presents limited direct investment exposure but serves as a leading indicator for humanitarian crisis insurance demand and food commodity price disruption.
actions: Monitor food commodity price impact from Sudan humanitarian corridor disruption
Assess African frontier market bond exposure for regional contagion risk
